results.possibleIdentity: Gary Allen is a Hudson Valley-based artist, photographer, and food historian who was part of the 'Golden Age of New Paltz' in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While studying at SUNY New Paltz, he became a close collaborator of Robert Schuler, a professor who was both a scientist and an artist. Together, they were involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that promoted collaborations between artists and engineers. Allen's early work incorporated scientific and mathematical elements, such as using mathematics to generate color sequences for paintings and designing custom lighting systems. He and Schuler also operated a business venture involving experimental architecture, creating buildings made from sprayed urethane foam. Over his long career, Allen has been a painter, illustrator, and postal artist, and his work has been featured in various exhibitions, including the 'Golden Age of New Paltz' retrospective. In his later career, he became a prolific writer and food historian, authoring or contributing to over 40 books, including 'Herbs: A Global History' and 'Sausage: A Global History', and he maintains the food history resource website 'On the Table'.

Gary Pembroke Allen (1937–2018) was a California-based artist, educator, and filmmaker active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He studied under Richard Diebenkorn at the San Francisco Art Institute and completed an MFA at San Francisco State University. His early work was associated with the Visionary and California Abstract Expressionist movements. Allen had a diverse career that included working as an illustrator for Walt Disney and serving as an Emmy-winning art director for television and film, notably on the series 'The X-Files'. He also taught painting, art history, and experimental filmmaking at San Francisco City College for many years. While his work in experimental film aligned with the artistic trends of the era, there is no direct mention of his involvement with the E.A.T. organization in available records.

results.possibleIdentity: Jack Perlmutter (1920–2006) was an American painter, printmaker, and educator who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in Washington, D.C., he served as the Chairman of the Graphics Department at the Corcoran Gallery School of Art from 1960 to 1982. He is notably recognized as a NASA artist, having been commissioned by the agency to document significant space missions, including the Saturn V, Apollo 6, and Apollo 16 missions in the late 1960s and early 1970s. His work, which often focused on urban scenes and technological achievements, is included in major collections such as the National Gallery of Art, the Smithsonian American Art Museum, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art. His participation in the NASA Art Program represents a notable intersection of art, science, and technology from the era, paralleling the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Jacob Perlmutter is a contemporary British documentary filmmaker, street photographer, and musician. He is best known for co-directing the award-winning feature documentary 'Two Strangers Trying Not to Kill Each Other' (2024) alongside Manon Ouimet. This film profiles the late-life relationship of the renowned photographer Joel Meyerowitz (who was prominent in the 1960s and 1970s) and artist Maggie Barrett. While this Jacob Perlmutter has been active recently, producing music videos, short films (such as 'French Exchange', 2017), and photography for institutions like the Tate Modern, his connection to the 1960s and 1970s is primarily through his documentary subjects and his archival-focused photography.

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Newman is a contemporary British photographer and artist who has produced work featuring technology-themed subjects such as circuit boards (e.g., "Digital World") and industrial landmarks (e.g., "Empire View"). His work is often available on platforms like Fine Art America and Pixels. While he is active today, some of his photographic subjects and styles evoke the aesthetics of the 1960s and 1970s. However, no direct, verified historical link between this specific photographer and the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was confirmed in the search results.

Martin Newman was an influential figure in the film exhibition industry during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He served as the Vice-President and Treasurer of Century Theatres and was the Chairman of the National Association of Theatre Owners (NATO) Pay-TV Committee (PTC). In this role, he led national efforts against the emergence of pay television and cable services between 1969 and 1973. While his work was primarily in the business and advocacy side of cinema, his activity aligns with the specified time period and the technological intersections of the era.

Martin Newman is a British arts critic, journalist, and editor who has written extensively on the arts, including a long-running arts blog for the Daily Mirror. He has reviewed a wide range of contemporary and historical art exhibitions and has served as a judge for prestigious art competitions such as the Derwent Drawing Prize. While he is an active voice in the 21st-century art world, his professional focus often involves contextualizing 20th-century movements like Abstract Expressionism and Minimalism.

Martin Newman is a specialist in heritage information and digital archaeology who has worked for Historic England (formerly English Heritage) for several decades. He is known for his work on the National Heritage List for England and has authored guidance on Historic Environment Records (HERs). His career has focused on the intersection of historical preservation and information technology, particularly in the digital management of archaeological data.

results.possibleIdentity: Eve Anne Feret (1944–2022) was an artist and physician who was active in the New York art scene in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She graduated from The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art in 1968 with a degree in art. During this period, she was part of the creative community in New York City, and her timeframe and academic background align with the active period of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization which many Cooper Union students and alumni collaborated with. In 1970, she was attending New York University, where she was described as an art major before pursuing a career in medicine. She eventually earned a B.S. from NYU, receiving two Founder's Day awards for academic achievement, and went on to graduate from the Universidad Autonoma de Guadalajara Medical School in Mexico. Dr. Feret practiced medicine for over 40 years in New Jersey, serving on the medical staffs of St. James and Columbus Hospitals in Newark and the Cooper Barnabas Medical Center in Livingston. Throughout her life, she remained an accomplished artist alongside her medical career.

segment.text: In 1970, she was attending New York University, where she was described as an art major before pursuing a career in medicine.
She eventually earned a B.S. from NYU, receiving two Founder's Day awards for academic achievement, and went on to graduate from the Universidad Autonoma de Guadalajara Medical School in Mexico.
